steps.title: Identify the chemical signature the test produced
steps.content: The passage draws a clear contrast: mineral-based ocher gets its color from iron oxide, while plant-based pigments contain a high level of carbon. The 2023 test of the Natufian beads found no iron but a high level of carbon—the exact chemical fingerprint of a plant-based pigment, not ocher.

steps.title: Match the chemical evidence to each conclusion
steps.content:
• Choice A — the Natufian beads examined in the study are the oldest surviving examples of the use of plant-based pigments for decorating beads.: The test shows what kind of pigment was used; it says nothing about whether older examples exist elsewhere. ✗
  • Choice B — the Natufian beadmakers used plant-based pigments rather than ocher to decorate some of the beads examined in the study.: No iron rules out ocher; high carbon confirms a plant-based source. This follows directly from the chemical data. ✓
  • Choice C — the Natufian beadmakers preferred to use plant-based pigments because they are much brighter than mineral-based pigments are.: The passage never compares brightness of pigment types. ✗
  • Choice D — the pigments used by the Natufian beadmakers likely came from plants because ocher was difficult to find.: The test reveals pigment chemistry, not local availability of materials. ✗
B is correct.
